variables.content:
Let $\Delta\rho(T)=A(B)T^{2}$ denote the low-temperature quasiparticle contribution to the resistivity in a Landau Fermi liquid and let $\gamma_{0}\equiv C/T$ denote the low-temperature specific-heat coefficient. The proposition is: in heavy-fermion and strongly correlated Fermi liquids of the type considered, the resistivity prefactor $A(B)$ scales with the quasiparticle effective mass according to $A(B)\propto\big(M^{*}(B)\big)^{2}$, consistent with empirical Kadowaki–Woods phenomenology and Fermi-liquid scattering-phase-space arguments, at least in the regime where quasiparticles remain well defined.
      K. Kadowaki and S.B. Woods, Solid State Commun. 58, 507 (1986); V.A. Khodel and P. Schuck, Z. Phys. B 104, 505 (1997)

Define the Fermi-liquid electrical resistivity prefactor A via the low-temperature expansion ρ = ρ₀ + A T² where ρ is expressed in μΩ·cm and T in kelvin, and define the Sommerfeld electronic specific-heat coefficient γ via C_p = γ T with γ expressed in J·mol⁻¹·K⁻². The empirical Kadowaki–Woods relation asserts that for many heavy-fermion compounds the ratio
      A / γ² ≈ 10⁻⁵ μΩ·cm · (mol·K / mJ)²,
      and this numerical proportionality is used here to convert a measured A into an estimate of γ, and hence into an estimate of the quasiparticle effective mass m (via γ ∝ m). This conversion is taken to be sufficiently accurate for the class of heavy-fermion materials analyzed that it does not introduce errors larger than the quoted uncertainties.
      Kadowaki & Woods, Solid State Commun. 1986; see also refs. in the paper.

The empirical Kadowaki–Woods relation, which in classic heavy-fermion compounds asserts an approximate proportionality A/γ^2 ≈ 1×10^−5 μΩ·cm·(mol·K/mJ)^2 between the coefficient A of the low-temperature resistivity term ρ(T) = ρ0 + A T^2 and the electronic specific-heat coefficient γ (where γ is the coefficient of the electronic linear-in-temperature term C_e = γT in the specific heat), is here treated as sufficient evidence that the specimen Pr(Cu,Ga)_{12.85} is a heavy-fermion compound with substantially enhanced quasiparticle effective mass m* when the experimentally measured values of A and γ fall within the empirical scatter of that Kadowaki–Woods ratio; this premise therefore equates numeric consistency with the Kadowaki–Woods relation to the interpretive classification "heavy-fermion" without requiring additional spectroscopic or thermodynamic confirmation of mass enhancement.
      Kadowaki, K. & Woods, S. B., Solid State Commun. 58 (1986) 507–509; CHO et al., Chem. Mater. 21 (2009) 3072-3078.

Comparing the fitted Fermi-liquid transport coefficient $A=0.138\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}$ with the measured Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma=159.77\ \mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}}$ yields an $A/\gamma^{2}$ value that lies near the empirical Kadowaki–Woods scaling band $A/\gamma^{2}\sim 10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\text{-cm}\ \mathrm{K^{-2}}\ (\mathrm{mJ\,mol^{-1}\,K^{-2}})^{-2}$ often observed for heavy-fermion materials, and therefore the transport and heat-capacity data of this compound are consistent with enhanced effective mass and strong electronic correlations within the usual empirical uncertainty of that relation.

The empirical Kadowaki–Woods relation A\propto \gamma_{e}^{2}, where A is the coefficient of the low-temperature resistivity \rho=\rho_{0}+AT^{2} and \gamma_{e} is the electronic Sommerfeld coefficient, is assumed to remain approximately valid across the valence-crossover regime in Ce- and Yb-based heavy fermion systems, so that a sharp reduction of \gamma_{e} (via a sharp reduction in the effective mass m^{*} associated with a change in n_{f}) produces a commensurate sharp decrease in A as observed experimentally.
      Kadowaki and Woods relation as discussed in the paper and refs [13]

The interpretation adopted for the Fermi-liquid coefficient A is that, in heavy-electron metals of the class that includes UGe₂, A scales approximately as (m*)² up to material-dependent constants (an empirical Kadowaki–Woods type relation), where m* denotes the quasiparticle effective mass characterizing low-energy electronic excitations; consequently, an observed increase of A with pressure or applied field is taken here to imply an increase of the quasiparticle effective mass m*.

For a two-band metal with one band having effective mass $m_1\sim 20m_{\mathrm{e}}$ and another having effective mass $m_2\sim 0.6m_{\mathrm{e}}$, the Kadowaki–Woods ratio defined as $A/\gamma_{\mathrm{el}}^2$ (with $\rho(T)=\rho_0+AT^2$ and $\gamma_{\mathrm{el}}$ the electronic Sommerfeld coefficient) naturally takes an intermediate magnitude between the canonical heavy-fermion value $\sim 1.0\times 10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\ \mathrm{cm}\ (\mathrm{mol}\ \mathrm{K}^{2}\ \mathrm{mJ})^{-2}$ and the typical transition-metal value $\sim 0.9\times 10^{-6}\ \mu\Omega\ \mathrm{cm}\ (\mathrm{mol}\ \mathrm{K}^{2}\ \mathrm{mJ})^{-2}$ because the heavy band strongly enhances both $\gamma_{\mathrm{el}}$ and electron–electron scattering while the light band partially dilutes the overall enhancement.
      Kadowaki, K., Woods, S.B.: Solid State Commun. 58, 507 (1986)

The Sommerfeld coefficient $\gamma$ used in subsequent estimates of Fermi wavevector, effective mass, and transport parameters is taken from the non‑quantum‑critical contribution $\gamma_{\mathrm{KW}}^{0\mathrm{T}}$ obtained by inverting the empirical Kadowaki–Woods relation $A/\gamma^2 = 10^{-5}\ \mu\Omega\mathrm{cm}\,(\mathrm{mol\,K})^2/(\mathrm{mJ})^2$, where $A$ is the measured Fermi‑liquid $A$ coefficient of $\rho=\rho_0+AT^2$; the proposition is that this Kadowaki–Woods‑derived $\gamma_{\mathrm{KW}}^{0\mathrm{T}}$ provides a representative estimate of the non‑quantum‑critical electronic density‑of‑states parameter suitable for use in the subsequent Drude‑informed estimates of $k_{\mathrm{F}}$, effective mass $m$, and transport scattering lengths in these Yb‑based heavy‑fermion single crystals.

Combining the experimentally determined low-temperature resistivity coefficient A = 2.57×10^−3 μΩ·cm·K^−2 and the Sommerfeld coefficient γ = 23.4 mJ mol^−1 K^−2 yields the empirical Kadowaki–Woods ratio A/γ^2 ≈ 0.5×10^−5 μΩ·cm·(mol·K/mJ)^2, comparable to the original Kadowaki–Woods scale observed in many heavy-fermion and valence-fluctuating systems. Using the low-temperature impurity-corrected molar susceptibility value χ_FL ≈ 5.6×10^−4 emu mol^−1 and the measured γ in the Sommerfeld–Wilson ratio defined as R_W = (π^2 k_B^2 χ_FL) / (γ μ_eff^2) (with k_B the Boltzmann constant and μ_eff the effective moment) gives R_W ≈ 0.81 when μ_eff = 2.54 μ_B (the free-ion Ce^{3+} moment) is used, or R_W ≈ 1.7 when μ_eff = 1.73 μ_B (a conduction-electron reference value) is used; both R_W estimates lie in the range expected for correlated Fermi liquids and are therefore consistent with Fermi-liquid phenomenology in the low-temperature state.

Define the low-temperature electrical resistivity increment Δρ(T,B) by Δρ(T,B)=A(B)T^{2} where A(B) is a transport prefactor depending on magnetic field B, and define the low-temperature specific-heat coefficient γ_0(B)=\lim_{T\to 0}C(T,B)/T; for a quasiparticle description of a heavy-fermion electronic liquid the prefactors obey the proportionalities
      $$
      A(B)\;\propto\;\big(M^{*}(B)\big)^{2},\qquad \gamma_0(B)\;\propto\;M^{*}(B),
      $$
      where M^{*}(B) is the quasiparticle effective mass at field B, so that the combination K\equiv A/\gamma_0^2 is approximately independent of M^{*} up to degeneracy and numerical prefactors.
      Empirical Kadowaki–Woods relation; cited theory Refs. in paper
